questions.questionText: The inguinal canal in males transmits the:
questions.explanation: The inguinal canal in males transmits the spermatic cord (containing vas deferens, testicular artery, pampiniform plexus, cremasteric artery, artery of vas, genital branch of genitofemoral nerve, ilioinguinal nerve on the cord, processus vaginalis remnant, lymphatics). In females it transmits the round ligament of uterus. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Inguinal Canal.

questions.questionText: The deep inguinal ring is located:
questions.optionA: Above and medial to the pubic tubercle
questions.optionB: Lateral to the inferior epigastric artery
questions.optionC: Medial to the inferior epigastric artery
questions.explanation: The deep inguinal ring is an opening in the transversalis fascia, situated lateral to the inferior epigastric artery (midpoint of inguinal ligament). The superficial ring is above and medial to the pubic tubercle. KEY DISTINCTION: Indirect inguinal hernia enters lateral to inferior epigastric artery (through deep ring), direct hernia is medial to it (Hesselbach's triangle). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Anterior Abdominal Wall.

questions.questionText: Hesselbach's triangle is bounded by all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Hesselbach's triangle boundaries: (1) Lateral — inferior epigastric artery, (2) Medial — lateral border of rectus abdominis, (3) Inferior — inguinal ligament. Transversalis fascia forms the floor (not a boundary). Direct inguinal hernia passes through Hesselbach's triangle (medial to inferior epigastric artery). EXAM TRAP: Transversalis fascia is the floor, not the boundary. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Anterior Abdominal Wall.

questions.questionText: The most common type of inguinal hernia is:
questions.explanation: Indirect inguinal hernia is the most common type overall (both sexes). It follows the path of the spermatic cord through the deep ring, inguinal canal, and may reach the scrotum. It is due to a patent processus vaginalis (congenital). Direct hernias push through Hesselbach's triangle (acquired, elderly). Femoral hernia is MC in females among groin hernias but overall inguinal is still more common. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Inguinal Region.

questions.questionText: McBurney's point is located at the junction of:
questions.optionA: Medial 2/3 and lateral 1/3 of a line from ASIS to umbilicus
questions.optionB: Lateral 2/3 and medial 1/3 of a line from ASIS to umbilicus
questions.optionC: Middle of a line from ASIS to umbilicus
questions.optionD: Lateral 1/3 and medial 2/3 of a line from umbilicus to pubic symphysis
questions.explanation: McBurney's point = junction of lateral 1/3 and medial 2/3 of a line joining the right ASIS to the umbilicus. It marks the base of the appendix. Maximum tenderness at this point suggests acute appendicitis. The incision for appendicectomy (McBurney's/Gridiron) is made perpendicular to this line at McBurney's point. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Appendix.

questions.questionText: The transpyloric plane (of Addison) is at the level of:
questions.explanation: Transpyloric plane (L1 level, midway between jugular notch and pubic symphysis). Important structures at L1: pylorus of stomach, fundus of gallbladder, hilum of kidneys, neck of pancreas, origin of SMA, termination of spinal cord (conus medullaris), cisterna chyli, duodenojejunal flexure, hilum of spleen. Memory aid: 'Everything happens at L1.' Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Abdominal Planes.

questions.questionText: The superior mesenteric artery supplies all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: SMA supplies the midgut derivatives: duodenum (distal to bile duct opening), jejunum, ileum, cecum, appendix, ascending colon, and proximal 2/3 of transverse colon. The descending colon, sigmoid colon, and distal 1/3 of transverse colon are supplied by the IMA (hindgut). The watershed area at the splenic flexure is the marginal artery of Drummond region. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Mesenteric Arteries.

questions.questionText: The portal vein is formed by the union of:
questions.optionA: Splenic vein and inferior mesenteric vein
questions.optionB: Superior mesenteric vein and splenic vein
questions.optionD: Splenic vein and left gastric vein
questions.explanation: Portal vein is formed behind the neck of the pancreas by the union of the superior mesenteric vein and splenic vein. The IMV usually drains into the splenic vein (or SMV or at the junction). The portal vein drains into the liver sinusoids and contains ~75% of hepatic blood flow. Portal pressure >12 mmHg = portal hypertension. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Portal Venous System.

questions.questionText: The most important site of portosystemic anastomosis causing esophageal varices is at the:
questions.explanation: Lower esophageal portosystemic anastomosis: Left gastric vein (portal) ↔ esophageal veins → azygos vein (systemic). In portal hypertension, this causes esophageal varices — the most clinically dangerous site (life-threatening hematemesis). Other sites: rectum (hemorrhoids), umbilicus (caput medusae), bare area of liver, retroperitoneum. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Portal System Anastomoses.

questions.questionText: The bare area of the liver is in direct contact with the:
questions.explanation: The bare area of the liver (on the posterior surface) is not covered by peritoneum and is in direct contact with the diaphragm. It is bounded by the superior and inferior layers of the coronary ligament. The right adrenal and right kidney are related to this area but separated by the diaphragm. Hepatic abscess can spread through the bare area to the pleural cavity. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Liver Peritoneal Relations.

questions.questionText: Calot's triangle is bounded by:
questions.optionA: Cystic duct, common hepatic duct, and liver edge
questions.optionB: Cystic duct, CBD, and cystic artery
questions.optionC: Common hepatic duct, CBD, and liver
questions.optionD: Cystic duct, common hepatic duct, and gallbladder
questions.explanation: Calot's triangle (hepatobiliary triangle): bounded by cystic duct (inferiorly), common hepatic duct (medially), and inferior surface of liver (superiorly). It contains the cystic artery (usually from right hepatic artery), cystic lymph node (Lund's node), and sometimes accessory hepatic ducts. Critical landmark during cholecystectomy to safely identify the cystic artery. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Biliary System.

questions.questionText: The second part of the duodenum has which structure opening into it?
questions.optionB: Main pancreatic duct and CBD at the ampulla of Vater
questions.explanation: The second (descending) part of the duodenum receives the main pancreatic duct (of Wirsung) and the common bile duct (CBD), which open together at the major duodenal papilla (ampulla of Vater, at the posteromedial wall). The accessory pancreatic duct (of Santorini) opens at the minor papilla (2 cm above). The sphincter of Oddi controls flow. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Duodenum.

questions.questionText: The epiploic foramen (of Winslow) is bounded posteriorly by the:
questions.explanation: Epiploic foramen boundaries: Anterior — free edge of lesser omentum (containing portal vein, hepatic artery, CBD), Posterior — IVC, Superior — caudate lobe of liver, Inferior — first part of duodenum. It connects the greater sac to the lesser sac (omental bursa). Pringle's maneuver: compressing the free edge of lesser omentum to control hepatic bleeding. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Peritoneal Folds.

questions.questionText: The relationship of structures in the free edge of the lesser omentum from anterior to posterior is:
questions.explanation: In the free edge of the lesser omentum (hepatoduodenal ligament), from anterior to posterior: CBD (right), Hepatic artery proper (left), Portal vein (posterior). Memory aid: 'Door-Bell-Porter' — Duct (anterior right), artery (Bell, anterior left), Portal vein (Porter, posterior). This arrangement is critical during biliary surgery. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Lesser Omentum.

questions.questionText: The spleen is related to which ribs?
questions.explanation: Spleen lies along the long axis of the 9th, 10th, and 11th ribs on the left side. Its long axis corresponds to the 10th rib. Memory for spleen: 1-3-5-7-9-11 (1 inch × 3 inches × 5 inches, weighs 7 oz, lies deep to 9-11 ribs). It is entirely intraperitoneal and attached by lienorenal and gastrosplenic ligaments. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Spleen.

questions.questionText: The most common position of the appendix is:
questions.explanation: Retrocecal position is the most common position of the appendix (~65-70%). In this position, classical signs of appendicitis may be masked (psoas sign positive). Pelvic (~30%) is the second most common. Preileal and postileal are uncommon. The appendix artery (from ileocolic artery) is an end artery — thrombosis leads to gangrene. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Appendix.

questions.questionText: The watershed area of the colon (Griffith's point) is located at the:
questions.explanation: Griffith's point (splenic flexure) = junction of SMA and IMA territories. Sudeck's point = rectosigmoid junction (IMA and internal iliac territories). These watershed/critical points are vulnerable to ischemia during low-flow states (e.g., AAA repair, hypotension). Ischemic colitis most commonly affects the splenic flexure. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Colon Blood Supply.

questions.questionText: The kidney is typically related posteriorly to which structures?
questions.optionA: Diaphragm, psoas major, quadratus lumborum, transversus abdominis
questions.explanation: Posterior relations of the kidney (from medial to lateral): psoas major, quadratus lumborum, transversus abdominis aponeurosis. The upper pole is also related to the diaphragm. The subcostal (T12), iliohypogastric (L1), and ilioinguinal (L1) nerves run posterior to the kidney. The right kidney is lower than the left (due to liver). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Kidney Relations.

questions.questionText: The left suprarenal (adrenal) gland is:
questions.explanation: Left adrenal gland is semilunar/crescentic. Right adrenal gland is pyramidal/triangular. The right adrenal vein drains directly into IVC (short, important during right adrenalectomy). Left adrenal vein drains into left renal vein. Arterial supply: superior (inferior phrenic), middle (aorta), inferior (renal artery). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Adrenal Glands.

questions.questionText: A 40-year-old man presents with a swelling in the right groin that descends into the scrotum. On examination, it is reducible and you can get above the swelling. It appears lateral to the inferior epigastric artery on imaging. The diagnosis is:
questions.explanation: Key features: descends into scrotum (only indirect hernia enters the scrotum through the deep ring following the spermatic cord), lateral to inferior epigastric artery (confirms indirect). Direct hernia: medial to inferior epigastric, rarely enters scrotum. Femoral hernia: below and lateral to pubic tubercle. Hydrocele: cannot get above it, transilluminant. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Hernias.

questions.questionText: The nerve most at risk during open appendicectomy through a McBurney's incision is:
questions.explanation: The ilioinguinal nerve (L1) runs between the internal oblique and transversus abdominis, passing through the inguinal canal. It is at greatest risk during a McBurney's (Gridiron) incision for appendicectomy. Damage causes loss of sensation over the inguinal region and medial thigh, and weakness of internal oblique. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Anterior Abdominal Wall Nerves.

questions.questionText: The uncinate process of the pancreas is related to which vessel?
questions.explanation: The uncinate process of the pancreas hooks behind the superior mesenteric vessels (SMA and SMV). This relationship is surgically important during Whipple's procedure (pancreaticoduodenectomy). The head of the pancreas lies within the C-loop of the duodenum. The splenic artery runs along the superior border of the pancreatic body/tail. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Pancreas.

questions.questionText: The celiac trunk branches into all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Celiac trunk (T12 level) has 3 branches: Left gastric artery (smallest, runs in lesser curvature), Common hepatic artery (gives gastroduodenal and proper hepatic arteries), Splenic artery (tortuous course along upper border of pancreas). SMA arises separately from the aorta at L1 level. The celiac trunk supplies the foregut derivatives. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Celiac Trunk.

questions.questionText: The part of the duodenum that crosses the midline is:
questions.explanation: The third (horizontal/transverse) part of the duodenum crosses the midline at L3 level, passing between the SMA (anteriorly) and aorta (posteriorly). It can be compressed between these two vessels — Superior Mesenteric Artery (SMA) syndrome (cast syndrome, Wilkie syndrome). The 4th part ascends to the duodenojejunal flexure (supported by the ligament of Treitz). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Duodenum.

questions.questionText: The ligament of Treitz suspends the:
questions.explanation: The ligament of Treitz (suspensory ligament of duodenum) is a musculofibrous band connecting the duodenojejunal flexure to the right crus of the diaphragm. It is the anatomical landmark dividing upper GI tract from lower GI tract. Upper GI bleeding = proximal to ligament of Treitz (hematemesis, melena). Lower GI bleeding = distal (hematochezia). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Duodenojejunal Junction.

questions.questionText: The left renal vein receives all the following tributaries EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Left renal vein receives: left suprarenal vein, left gonadal vein (testicular/ovarian), and left inferior phrenic vein. It crosses anterior to the aorta (can be compressed — Nutcracker syndrome). Right gonadal vein drains directly into the IVC (not the right renal vein). This is why left varicocele is more common (longer drainage path, right-angle entry). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Renal Veins.

questions.questionText: Meckel's diverticulum follows the 'rule of 2s.' Which of the following is NOT part of this rule?
questions.explanation: Meckel's diverticulum rule of 2s: 2% of population, 2 feet (~60cm) from ileocecal valve, 2 inches long, 2 types of ectopic tissue (gastric MC, pancreatic), presents in first 2 years of life, 2:1 male to female ratio (NOT more common in females). It is the most common congenital anomaly of the GI tract, remnant of the vitellointestinal duct. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Embryology of GI Tract.

questions.questionText: The retroperitoneal organs include all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Retroperitoneal organs (mnemonic SAD PUCKER): Suprarenal glands, Aorta/IVC, Duodenum (2nd-4th parts), Pancreas (except tail), Ureters, Colon (ascending/descending), Kidneys, Esophagus (thoracic), Rectum (lower 2/3). The sigmoid colon has a mesentery (sigmoid mesocolon) and is intraperitoneal. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Peritoneum.

questions.questionText: During right nephrectomy, the structure crossing anterior to the right renal artery is:
questions.explanation: The right renal vein crosses anterior to the right renal artery to drain into the IVC. On the left side, the left renal vein crosses anterior to the aorta. The right renal artery is longer (crosses behind the IVC) while the left renal artery is shorter. The right renal vein is shorter than the left. This asymmetry is important for living donor nephrectomy (left kidney preferred — longer vein). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Kidney Vasculature.

questions.questionText: The narrowest part of the ureter is at:
questions.explanation: The ureter has 3 normal constrictions where stones tend to lodge: (1) Pelviureteric junction (PUJ), (2) Pelvic brim (where ureter crosses iliac vessels), (3) Vesicoureteric junction (VUJ) — the narrowest. The most common site for a stone to get impacted is the VUJ. The ureter is 25 cm long, lies on the psoas muscle, and crosses the bifurcation of the common iliac artery. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Ureter.

questions.questionText: The 'gastric bed' forming the posterior relations of the stomach includes all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: The stomach bed (posterior relations, separated by lesser sac): left crus of diaphragm, left adrenal gland, upper part of left kidney, splenic artery, pancreas, transverse mesocolon, and splenic flexure of colon. The RIGHT adrenal gland is NOT part of the stomach bed. EXAM TRAP: Left adrenal is a posterior relation, right adrenal is NOT. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Stomach Relations.

questions.questionText: Which part of the colon has teniae coli, appendices epiploicae, and haustra?
questions.explanation: The distinctive features of the large intestine (colon) are teniae coli (3 bands of longitudinal muscle), haustra (sacculations), and appendices epiploicae (fat-filled peritoneal tags). These features are present in the cecum, ascending, transverse, descending, and sigmoid colon. The rectum does NOT have teniae coli or appendices epiploicae. The appendix has a complete longitudinal muscle coat. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Large Intestine.

questions.questionText: The segment of the liver that can be safely resected without compromising the hepatic vascular supply is:
questions.explanation: The left lateral segment (Couinaud segments II and III) can be safely resected (left lateral sectionectomy) as it has an independent portal pedicle and hepatic venous drainage. The caudate lobe (segment I) receives blood from both right and left branches and drains directly into the IVC — it is spared in Budd-Chiari syndrome (caudate lobe hypertrophy). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Liver Anatomy Couinaud.
